topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm. I reread the text, the words blurring slightly at the edges, trying to find a logical flaw, a typo that would explain the impossible. The scholarship, the one I’d worked relentlessly for, was designated for 'Elias Vance.' Elias Vance. My name was listed as a placeholder, a clerical error, a ghost in the system. I stared at the screen, my fingers trembling so violently I had to press them flat against the desk to steady them. A cold, creeping sensation began to snake its way up my spine, chilling me despite the stuffy warmth of my dorm room. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small space, the floorboards creaking under my weight. Every shadow seemed to deepen, and the silence of the room amplified the frantic thumping inside my chest.

[story 2]

I kept catching myself staring at the acceptance letter, my eyes scanning the fine print again and again. It wasn't a mistake; it was a fundamental misalignment of reality. The weight of the deception settled on my shoulders, heavy and suffocating. I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over the contact list, but I couldn't bring myself to call anyone. The air in the library felt suddenly thin, devoid of any comforting familiarity. I walked to the window, looking out at the bustling campus, the vibrant energy of students moving about, yet I felt utterly isolated, a strange, hollow space opening up inside my chest. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the image of the document away, but the details—the names, the dates, the sheer unfairness of it—kept replaying behind my eyelids, leaving a residue of unease that clung to my skin.

[story 3]

When the professor finally explained the discrepancy, the words seemed to arrive in slow motion. I sat frozen in my chair, the sound of the lecture dissolving into a dull roar. My hands, resting on my notebook, felt strangely numb. I looked down at my hands, noticing the slight tremor that had started in my fingers and now seemed to vibrate constantly. A profound sense of disorientation washed over me, as if the ground beneath my feet had shifted. I tried to formulate a response, to ask for clarification, but my throat constricted. I could only manage a dry, rasping sound. The world outside the lecture hall seemed overly bright, the colors too sharp, and I felt an overwhelming urge to simply retreat, to become invisible, to disappear into the fabric of the room until the unsettling realization faded.
